text.title: perf-test-basics Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for perf-test-basics. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Use load tests to verify system behavior under expected traffic; run for an extended period (hours) at normal-to-peak users.
rule.rationale: Load tests establish the production-shaped baseline and confirm SLOs hold under representative concurrency.

rule.statement: Use stress tests to find the breaking point; run until failure with users exceeding maximum expected capacity.
rule.rationale: Knowing where the system breaks informs autoscaling thresholds and circuit-breaker settings.

rule.statement: Use spike tests to validate behavior under sudden load jumps (low to high in seconds); run in short bursts.
rule.rationale: Marketing campaigns and viral events produce spike shapes, not gradual ramps.

rule.statement: Use endurance (soak) tests at normal load for 12-72 hours to detect memory leaks and resource exhaustion.
rule.rationale: Memory leaks and file-descriptor leaks are invisible in short runs; only sustained load makes the trend measurable.

rule.statement: Use scalability tests with incrementally increasing users to verify that horizontal/vertical scaling produces proportional throughput gains.
rule.rationale: A system that does not scale linearly has a bottleneck that must be found before capacity planning is trustworthy.

rule.statement: Record p50, p95, p99, error rate, RPS, concurrent users, and duration for every load-test run. A single average latency number lies.
rule.rationale: Tail latency (p99) is where users churn; aggregate averages hide bimodal distributions caused by slow endpoints or N+1 queries.

rule.statement: Never load-test from the same machine as the system under test — observer effect dominates the results.
rule.rationale: CPU and network contention on the test machine competes with the target, producing artificially low throughput numbers.

rule.statement: Warm caches and connection pools before measuring (one full pass at low concurrency) so JIT/connection-pool effects do not dominate.
rule.rationale: Cold-start numbers are not representative of sustained production behavior and will produce misleading baselines.

rule.statement: Store raw results (not summaries) so you can re-aggregate later with different percentile cuts.
rule.rationale: Summarized results cannot be re-analyzed; raw results survive SLO changes and retrospective root-cause work.

rule.statement: Run all load tests against a staging environment that mirrors production in hardware, data volume, and network topology.
rule.rationale: Perf numbers from a weak-hardware or empty-database environment are not transferable to production.

rule.statement: Hard-code an environment allowlist in every load-test script (e.g., assert "prod" not in TARGET_URL) to prevent accidental production load.
rule.rationale: Agents running automated load tests will target production by accident without this guard.

rule.statement: For Locust, enforce a gradual spawn-rate profile (e.g., 10 users/second) rather than setting spawn_rate = total_users.
rule.rationale: Spawning all users simultaneously overwhelms the target before the test starts, invalidating the measurement.

rule.statement: Use sampling profilers (py-spy, pyinstrument) on running services; use deterministic profilers (cProfile) on offline reproductions. Sampling overhead is bounded; deterministic overhead can distort results.
rule.rationale: On a running production-like service, cProfile's instrumentation overhead can exceed 30% and hide the real bottleneck.

rule.statement: Profile a single representative request before load-testing a change — flame graphs narrow hypothesis space before any optimization attempt.
rule.rationale: Load-testing without a flame graph leads to optimizing cold paths because they look messy, not because they are hot.

rule.statement: Use memray (not memory_profiler) for allocation tracking on the whole process; memory_profiler only instruments the decorated function and misses leaks elsewhere.
rule.rationale: memory_profiler's @profile decorator scope is too narrow for leak hunting; memray tracks all allocations process-wide.

rule.statement: For memory leaks, run an endurance test for hours and watch RSS slope over time — do not rely on single-snapshot peak values.
rule.rationale: A leak produces a gradual slope in RSS; a single snapshot cannot distinguish a one-time allocation spike from a continuous leak.

rule.statement: Sort cProfile stats by cumulative time (stats.sort_stats('cumulative')) and inspect the top 20 functions to find the dominant hotspot.
rule.rationale: Cumulative time shows total cost including callee chains; per-call time alone misses deep recursive or high-fanout call sites.

rule.statement: Use py-spy record -f speedscope for 30 seconds on the target PID, then open in speedscope.app to identify top-3 hot functions by total time.
rule.rationale: Speedscope's left-heavy view makes it easy to identify the dominant call tree without reading raw text output.

rule.statement: Simulate realistic network latency when profiling localhost (e.g., tc qdisc add dev lo root netem delay 20ms) to avoid concluding the service is fast due to zero-latency loopback.
rule.rationale: Without artificial latency, profiling results show CPU time only and hide serialization/IO-wait hotspots that dominate in production.

rule.statement: Run pytest-benchmark with --benchmark-min-rounds=20; the default rounds produce noisy results on short functions.
rule.rationale: Statistical noise in micro-benchmarks is dominated by system interrupts and GC pauses; more rounds reduce variance enough to distinguish signal from noise.

rule.statement: Gate CI on --benchmark-compare-fail=mean:5% against the previous main commit to catch performance regressions automatically.
rule.rationale: Without a CI gate, benchmark results accumulate in reports nobody reads; a fail threshold makes regressions visible at merge time.

rule.statement: Read benchmark stats.mean and stats.stddev together; agents that read mean and ignore stddev will merge regressions hidden by high variance.
rule.rationale: A benchmark with mean 10ms and stddev 8ms is not better than one with mean 12ms and stddev 0.5ms — the former result is unreliable.

rule.statement: Use an SQLAlchemy before_cursor_execute event listener fixture to count queries, not caplog log-scraping. caplog misses queries when log level is INFO.
rule.rationale: Log-level-dependent query counting is fragile; the event listener fires on every cursor execution regardless of log configuration.

rule.statement: Assert query_counter["n"] <= expected_count (typically 1-3 for a paginated list endpoint), not a static threshold like 5.
rule.rationale: A static threshold of 5 passes an N+1 that fires on every item in a list of 4; the threshold must be tied to the expected query plan.

rule.statement: Define per-endpoint thresholds (p95, error rate, minimum RPS) in a versioned config file; fail the CI pipeline when any threshold is breached.
rule.rationale: Reviewing perf graphs manually is unreliable; a failing CI step creates an actionable artifact tied to a specific commit.

rule.statement: Require human approval to increase a threshold; agents that push thresholds up after every regression to keep CI green destroy the regression signal.
rule.rationale: Threshold drift is the most common way performance monitoring becomes a rubber stamp.

rule.statement: Always capture: p50, p95, p99, error rate, RPS, concurrent users, duration. A single "average latency" number lies.
rule.rationale: Aggregate averages hide tail latency where users churn and bimodal distributions caused by slow code paths.

rule.statement: Compare runs on identical hardware/data — perf numbers are not transferable across environments.
rule.rationale: Different hardware, empty vs full data, and network topology produce incomparable baseline.

rule.statement: For micro-benchmarks, run pytest-benchmark with --benchmark-min-rounds=20; default rounds give noisy results.
rule.rationale: More rounds reduce variance enough to distinguish signal from noise.

rule.statement: Set explicit thresholds in CI per critical endpoint; fail the pipeline on regression rather than reviewing graphs.
rule.rationale: Manual review is unreliable; failing CI creates an actionable artifact tied to a commit.

rule.statement: Use sampling profilers (py-spy, pyinstrument) over deterministic (cProfile) on running services — overhead is bounded.
rule.rationale: Deterministic profilers can have 30%+ overhead and hide the real bottleneck.

rule.statement: For memory leaks, run an endurance test for hours and watch RSS slope, not single-snapshot peak.
rule.rationale: A leak produces gradual slope; single snapshots cannot distinguish spikes from continuous leaks.

rule.statement: Never load-test from the same machine as the system under test — observer effect dominates.
rule.rationale: CPU and network contention on test machine competes with target, producing artificially low throughput.
